themes.heading: 기본정보 (basisinformatie)
themes.text: ㅇ 공식 국명 : 이집트 아랍공화국(Arab Republic of Egypt) ※ 나세르 혁명의 날 : 1952.7.23 ㅇ 위치 : 동북 아프리카 지중해 및 홍해 연안에 위치하며 북위 20-30도, 동경 30도에 위치함 ㅇ 수도 : 카이로(Cairo 인구 약 1,050만 명, 2025.12월 기준) ㅇ 면적 : 100.1만 평방 킬로(한반도의 약 5배, 전국토의 95% 사막) ㅇ 인구 : 1억 2천만명(2026년.6월 기준) ㅇ 언어 : 아랍어(공용어), 영어 및 불어 제한적 사용(식자층) ㅇ 종교 : 이슬람교(90%-수니파), 기독교(대부분 콥트 정교)10% ㅇ 기후 : 고온 건조, 온화한 겨울 ㅇ 공휴일 : 금, 토(토요일 비 휴무 직종 다수 있음) ㅇ 시차 : GMT + 2시간(한국보다 7시간 늦음) ※일광절약제(26.4.24(금) ~ 26.10.30(금)) 실시로 인해 한국보다 6시간 늦음

themes.heading: 치안상황 및 사건ㆍ사고의 유형
themes.text: [외국인 대상 범죄 유형 및 여행시 주의사항] ㅇ 오토바이 날치기(보행중 휴대전화 및 소지품 등), 절도, 성희롱 및 성추행, 요금 시비 등 다양한 형태의 범죄에 대해 주의 필요 - 드물게, 무장 강도에 의한 차량 강탈, 강도 등 형태의 범죄도 발생 - 특히, 고가 스마트폰은 범죄 표적이 되기 쉽기 때문에 보행 중 휴대전화 사용 지양 ㅇ 주요 관광지에는 군인과 경찰, 사복 경찰이 배치되어 검문검색이 이뤄지고 있어 관광지 주변은 비교적 안전한 편 [높은 교통사고 발생률 등 교통사고 주의사항] ㅇ 과속 운전, 난폭 운전 사례들로 인한 교통사고 발생 주의 - 장거리 이동 시 반드시 탑승 예정인 차량의 안전벨트 유무를 확인하고, 차량 이동 시 안전벨트 상시 착용 - 도로를 걷거나, 횡단 시 교통사고 피해 발생치 않도록 스스로 각별한 주의 필요 ㅇ 도시 간 장거리 이동시 택시 이용 주의 - 도시 간 이동 시 장시간(5시간~10시간)이 소요되는 거리를 택시로 이동하는 경우, 택시 기사의 피로도 증가 등으로 교통사고 발생 주의 필요 - 장거리 이동 중 택시가 과속을 하다가 사고가 발생하거나, 차량 노후화로 인한 전복 사고 발생 등으로 중상 피해를 입는 경우가 많은바, 주의 필요 - 특히, 야간에 택시를 이용한 장거리 이동은 사고 발생 위험이 매우 높은바, 각별한 주의 필요(야간 장거리 이동은 자제하되, 부득이 야간 이동 필요시 정식 노선 버스 우선적으로 검토) - 승차 공유앱(우버, 인드라이브 등) 차량은 정식 허가 받은 택시가 아닌바, 도시 간 이동 시 검문검색 과정에서 미허가 여행객 수송으로 운전기사가 과태료를 받고, 이를 승객에게 전가하는 사례도 드물게 발생 [각종 테러 발생으로 테러 위협 상존] ㅇ 이집트는 2010년대 다수의 테러 상황이 발생하였으나, 2020년 이후 발생 사건 수 현격히 감소, 최근에는 테러 사건 발생 없었음 ㅇ 다만, 시나이 반도 북부 지역, 리비아 및 수단 국경 지역 등 여행경보 3단계(출국 권고) 지역은 테러 위험 상존 [주요 테러 사건] - 2019.8.5. 국립 암센터 앞 차량 폭발로 20명 사망 - 2019.4.9. 북 시나이주 Sheikh Zuweid 소재 시장에서 자살폭탄 테러로 경찰과 4명 및 민간인 3명 사망 - 2019.4.7. 카이로 el-Nozah 지역에서 총격테러로 경찰관 3명 사망 - 2019.2.18. 카이로 Al-Azhar 모스크에서 자살폭탄 테러로 2명 사망 - 2018.12.28. 기자 피라미드 인근 도로상 설치된 폭탄테러로 관광버스에 탑승한 베트남인 3명 포함 4명 사망 - 2017.11.24. Arish 지역 Al-Rawda 모스크에서 무차별 총기 테러로 311명 사망, 122명 부상 - 2017.5.26. Minya 지역에서 콥트교도 대상 총기 테러로 28명 사망, 26명 부상 - 2017.4.9. 알렉산드리와 탄타 지역 콥트교회 두 곳에서 연쇄적 폭탄테러로 최소 44명이 사망하고 126명이 다쳤음 - 2014.10.24. 시나이 반도 Arish 지역에서 차량 폭탄 테러로 이집트군 25명 사망 - 2014.2.16. 시나이 반도 타바 지역에서 한국인 성지순례 버스 차량에서 괴한에 의한 폭탄 테러로 한국인 3명을 포함 5명 사망, 14명 부상

themes.heading: 유의해야 할 지역
themes.text: [시나이 반도-다합 포함] ㅇ 지중해와 홍해 사이에 위치한 삼각형 모양의 반도로 2014년 2월 우리국민 3명이 사망하는 폭탄테러가 발생한 지역이기도 함 ㅇ 2018년 2월부터 이집트 정부군은 테러 단체 소탕을 위한 대테러 작전을 지속적으로 벌이고 있음 ㅇ 중·북부 시나이반도(1,2단계 지역 제외)는 출국권고 지역으로 지정되있음 -1단계 : 다합·샴엘셰이크 지역, 2단계 : 시나이반도 남부 일부 지역(성캐더린·타바) [이집트-리비아 접경 지역] ㅇ 우리 외교부는 현재(2026.6) 이집트-리비아 국경으로부터 30km까지 출국권고가 지정된 상황 [할라입 삼각지대] ㅇ 동 지역의 여행경보 표시 경계가 정확히 각 국의 국경선을 토대로 이루어진 것은 아님 ㅇ 동 지역에서 영사 조력이 필요한 경우 주이집트대사관으로 연락 요망 [주요 종교 시설] ㅇ 사람들이 많이 찾는 대중 밀집 시설 등이 주요 테러 공격 대상이 될 수 있으므로 주의가 필요함 [기타] ㅇ 카이로의 Muqattam 언덕은 카이로 시내의 야경을 볼수 있는 곳으로 야간에 외국인들이 많이 찾는 곳이나 이들을 상대로 강도행위가 자주 발생하고, Shoubra 지역 등 극빈층이 주로 거주하는 지역도 강도 및 절도 발생이 빈번하며, 나일강변에 위치한 주요 클럽 및 카지노 출입에도 각별히 유의가 필요함

themes.heading: 출입국 (in- en uitreis)
themes.text: 무비자 입국 가능 여부(1회 입국시 최대 연속 체류 가능 일자) ㅇ 외교관 여권 소지자 : O, (90일 협정) ㅇ 관용여권 소지자 : O, (90일 협정) ㅇ 일반여권 소지자(복수와 단수를 구분할 경우 별도 표기 필요) : X ㅇ 도착사증 발급 여부 : 도착사증 발급(30미불) ㅇ 시나이 반도(샤름엘셰이크 공항, 뉴웨이바 항구, 타바 육상 국경)로 입국시 무료 비자(14일)가 발급되나, 카이로 등 시나이 반도 외 지역으로 이동을 위해서는 반드시 도착사증(30미불)을 별도 구매 후 해당 비자로 입국절차 밟아야 함 ※ 타바 국경으로 입국 시 입국세(120미불), 보건 수수료(35이집트파운드), 현지 여행사의 보증서(20~30미불)가 별도로 부과됨 입국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 10,000불 이상 소지시 신고 의무 ㅇ 드론, 무전기, 고성능 쌍안경, 고성능 카메라 등 반입 금지 ㅇ 시나이 반도는 4륜구동차량의 진입이 제한됨 출국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 10,000불까지 소지 가능하며, 5,000 이상 소지시 신고 의무가 있고, 10,000을 초과한 금액에 대해서는 입국시 신고한 금액까지만 소지 가능 ㅇ 이집트 파운드화 5,000까지 소지 가능 참고자료 ㅇ 해당 국가의 주한공관홈페이지 ㅇ 해당국가의 한국 대사관 홈페이지 : https://www.overseas.mofa.go.kr/eg-ko/index.do

themes.heading: 일반 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 이집트는 고대에 파라오가 지배하는 국가였으나 중세에는 로마 및 이슬람의 지배를 받아 기독교 및 이슬람교의 영향을 받았고, 근대에는 서구 열강의 식민지배로 인하여 서양 문화를 받아들임에 따라 이슬람 종교적 특수성과 근대법 사상 제도가 섞여 있는 문화입니다.

themes.heading: 종교 관련
themes.text: ㅇ 이집트는 이슬람 국가이나 콥틱교회(기독교의 한 분파)를 인정하는 등 법령에 의해 개인의 종교 자유가 보장되고 외국인들의 종교활동(예배)에 특별한 제한은 없으며, 외국인이 많이 거주하는 지역(자말렉, 헬리오폴리스, 마아디 등)에는 성당, 개신교회 등이 있어 종교활동(예배)에 큰 불편은 없습니다. ㅇ 그러나 선교 활동은 인정하지 않으며, 특히 샤리아(이슬람 종교법)에 의해 무슬림에 대한 선교활동은 엄격히 제한되고, 선교활동 적발시에는 강제 추방 조치될 수 있음을 유의해야 합니다. ㅇ 대중 공공장소에서 노출이 과도한 옷을 삼가는 것이 필요하고, 현지 여성에게 먼저 악수를 청하는 등 불필요한 신체접촉을 하지 않는 것에 유의해야 합니다. ㅇ 또한 이슬람 종교를 모독하는 행위는 주재국 법령에 의해 처벌될 수 있습니다.

themes.heading: 팁 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 이집트에는 팁 문화가 널리 통용되므로 호텔에서의 서비스는 물론 식당, 주차장, 주유소 등에서 서비스를 받았을 때도 20-50파운드의 팁을 주는 문화 있습니다. ㅇ 호텔이나 식당 등에서는 계산서에 봉사료(12%) 및 부과세(14%)가 포함되어 있으나 이와 별도로 봉사한 직원이 팁을 기대하는 경우가 있습니다.

themes.heading: 주요 교통 법규 및 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 교통법규는 우리나라와 유사하나, 원형교차로가 많이 설치되어 있는 반면 신호등은 주요 교차로 외에는 설치되어 있지 않음 - 원형교차로의 경우 선진입 차량에 우선권이 있으나, 주재국은 동 규칙이 적용되지 않는 경우가 다반사 ㅇ 고속도로와 차량 전용 도로(카이로의 경우 링로드 포함)에서는 과속, 역주행 등 교통법규 위반 차량에 의한 대형 교통사고가 빈발하여 현지 운전 문화에 익숙하지 않은 단기 여행자의 경우 직접 운전을 삼가는 것이 좋음

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 사례
themes.text: [빈번한 교통사고 유형] ㅇ 도로가 잘 정비되어 있지 않으며, 갑작스러운 끼어듬, 무단횡단 등 장애요인이 많고, 부주의 과속 운전, 택시 내 안전벨트 미설치 등으로 교통사고가 빈발하고 있음 ※ 한국인 관광객이 공항에서 차량을 렌트하여 나오자마자 사고가 발생한 사례가 있음(운전시 한국에서의 예측가능성을 가지고 주재국에서 운전할 경우 사고발생 가능성 매우 높음) ㅇ 역주행 하는 차량도 많으며, 차량 고장으로 도로상에 주정차 하는 경우도 있으므로 운전시 전방 예의주시가 필요함 - 도로상에 말, 당나귀를 끌고 가는 마차들이 있으므로 주의가 요구됨 □ 교통 관련 주요 사건사고 [정차 차량 추돌 사망] ㅇ 2016.7 한국인 탑승 차량(현지인 운전)이 도로상에 정차해 있던 트럭을 추돌하여 한국인 1명 사망 및 1명 중상 [무단 횡단 보행자 사망] ㅇ 2019.12 한국인이 자동차 전용도로를 달리던 중 갑자기 도로로 나온 현지인 무단횡단자 2명을 치어 그 중 1명이 사망하는 사고 발생 [정면충돌 교통사고 중상] ㅇ 2025.3.21. 심야시간 대 현지 택시로 이동 중 정면충돌 교통사고가 발생하여 2명 중상 [끼어들기 등에 의한 사고] ㅇ 주재국은 차선이 없는 도로도 많고, 차선을 지키지 않는 차량들이 많아서 끼어들기로 인한 교통사고 발생 다발

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 발생시 대처방법
themes.text: ㅇ 교통사고 발생시 경찰(122)에 신고 - 교통사고 발생시에도 우리나라와는 달리 보험을 통해 보상을 받을 가능성은 높지 않고, 보상을 받는 경우에도 변호인을 선임하여 재판 절차를 진행해야 하므로, 대부분 현장에서 말다툼 또는 일정 보상금액을 협상한 후 사건을 종결하는 것이 일반화 - 따라서, 여행 중 교통사고 피해 예방을 위해 스스로 각별한 주의 필요 (여행자 보험 가입 적극 권장) □ 대중교통 [항공] ㅇ 국제선ㆍ국내선 모두 연발착이 잦은 편이고, 출국 시 공항 내 잦은 보안 검사와 교통사정을 감안하여 최소 3시간 전 공항에 도착하여 출국을 준비하는 것이 필요함. 카이로 국제공항은 3개의 터미널(각 터미널 간 이동 시 차량으로 5-10분 거리)이 있으므로 미리 출국 장소를 확인하는 것이 필요 (국내 장거리 이동 시 항공편 이용 이동 적극 권장) [철도] ㅇ 이집트 내 철도 시스템은 다소 노후화 되어 열차 추돌 등 인명사고가 자주 발생하고 있어 철도를 이용한 여행은 자제할 것을 권고 [택시] ㅇ 택시는 요금 미터기가 있어도 사용되지 않는 경우가 종종 있고(이집트인의 경우에도 동일), 외국인 승객에 대해서는 별도 요금을 요구하는 경우가 있음. 일반택시 이용 시 탑승 전 미리 흥정할 필요가 있으며, 요금은 목적지에 도착하여 하차 후에 지불하는 것이 필요함(요금 불만으로 하차시켜 주지 않거나 승객에게 곤란을 주는 경우 종종 발생) ㅇ 시내에서 일반 택시를 이용할 시 단거리(우리나라 기본요금 거리 정도)는 30-100파운드, 20-30분 이상의 운행거리의 경우 150-300파운드 가량이 소요됨 ㅇ 도시 내에서 우버, 인드라이브 등 차량 공유 어플리케이션을 이용하는 것이 요금 분쟁을 피할 수 있고 정확한 목적지 도착을 위해서도 일반 택시보다는 나은 편임 ※ 우리 주이집트대사관에 방문시, 현지 택시를 이용할 경우 택시 기사가 남한과 북한을 혼동하여 우리 여행객들을 북한 대사관으로 안내하는 경우도 발생한 적이 있으므로, 택시기사에게 대한민국 대사관으로 정확하게 설명할 필요가 있고 구글맵으로 위치 확인을 시켜주는 것이 필요함 [버스] ㅇ 버스는 미니버스, 시내버스, 장거리 버스 등으로 구분됨 - 미니버스는 일정 지역 구간을 운행하나 정해진 목적지가 없으며, 구간에 따라 요금이 차등 적용됨. 미니버스의 경우 과속으로 인해 대형 교통사고가 빈발하고, 승차 중 시비 등 발생 가능성이 있어 이용하지 않는 것이 좋음 - 시내버스의 경우 일정한 노선에 따라 운행되고 있으나, 이용객이 많고 언어 소통에 애로 발생 가능성이 높은 바, 이용 자제 필요 ㅇ 장거리 운행 버스(주로 GO Bus를 이용)는 카이로 시내 Tahrir 광장 및 람세스 역에서 출발하며 알렉산드리아, 포트사이드, 시나이 반도, 후르가다 등 주요 도시와 연결 운행되고 있음 ㅇ 카이로 시내에는 총 연장 50여 Km에 달하는 지하철이 운행 중으로 1-3호선이 있으며, 역 명칭이 영문 알파벳으로 표기되어 초행자의 경우도 이용이 비교적 편리한 편임. 지하철의 기본요금은 10EGP(이집트 파운드)이며 이용 거리에 따라 요금이 추가(12, 15, 20EGP)됨. 이슬람의 남녀 분리 관습에 따라 여성전용 객실을 운영하고 있는데 열차의 중간 부분에 위치하고 있음(대사관은 Dokki 역에서 도보로 5-10분 내 거리에 위치)

themes.heading: 기타
themes.text: [의료체계 및 주요 의료시설] ㅇ 일부 병원을 제외하고, 의료시설이 전반적으로 낙후되어 있고 위생관념이 낮으므로 가급적 현지에서 수술은 피하는 게 좋으며, 수술을 요하거나 장기치료 필요시 인근 국가(프랑스, 독일 등 유럽 국가)에서 치료를 받는 것이 일반적임(간단한 수술은 가능). 특히 외상, 골절상 등의 경우에는 반드시 의사소통이 가능한 전문의를 통해 치료받는 것을 권장 ㅇ 수돗물은 석회 함유량이 많기 때문에 가급적 생수를 구입, 음용하는 것이 필요함 ㅇ 이집트를 장기간 방문 시에는 가급적 건강진단을 받고 오는 것이 필요하며, 해열제, 물파스, 감기약, 소화제, 우황청심환, 진통제, 연고(벌레 물림 등), 안약 등 일반 상비약을 준비하는 것이 좋음. 현지 음식물로 인한 경미한 질환에 대한 약품은 의사 진료 처방 없이 약국에서 구매 가능하며, 가격도 저렴한 편임 중동 상황 관련 ㅇ 2026년 2월 중동 지역 내 상황이 격화되며, 카타르 항공, 에미레이트 항공, 에티하드 항공 등 항공사 운항 중단 사례가 있었던바, 이집트 여행 중 중동상황이 악화되는 경우 자신이 예약한 항공편이 정상 운항중인지 수시 확인 필요 ㅇ 일부 항공사가 운항을 중단하는 경우, 유럽 및 아프리카 지역을 경유하는 항공편 등 대체 항공편을 통해 국내 귀국 가능함 참고 필요

themes.heading: 날씨
themes.text: ㅇ 이집트의 기후는 아열대성 기후와 사막기후를 보이고 있으며 대체로 3가지 유형으로 분류됨 - 나일강 델타 지역 : 알렉산드리아 등 지중해 연안지역은 온화한 지중해성 기후이나 그 외 지역은 아열대성 기후 - 사막지대 : 국토의 95%를 차지하는 나일강 서편의 광대한 리비아 사막 지역, 동편의 길고 좁은 아라비아 사막 지역과 홍해를 건너 위치한 시나이 사막 지역 등으로 여름철 한낮의 기온이 섭씨 40도에서 50도 유지 ㅇ 이집트도 4계절이 있으나 봄, 가을은 매우 짧고, 12월∼2월은 겨울로 기온이 영하로 내려가지는 않으나 야간 체감온도가 낮으므로(특히 사막여행, 산행, 야외행사시) 적절한 겨울옷(내복, 점퍼, 파카, 겨울 양말)을 준비하는 것이 필요

themes.heading: Latest update
themes.text: Still current at:22 July 2026 Updated:17 June 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution in Egypt overall. There are higher levels in some areas. Terrorism is a threat, including in Egypt. Terrorist attacks could happen at any time or anywhere. Maintain a high level of security awareness. Avoid possible targets including venues that draw in foreigners, tourists or crowds where possible. If there's a security incident, follow the advice of local authorities. Demonstrations and protest activity may also occur, and local security situations could deteriorate with little notice. Avoid all demonstrations and protests (see 'Safety'). Egypt has increased health screening measures at airports and other ports of entry in response to the Ebola outbreak in parts of Africa. If you’re travelling from an Ebola affected country, you may need to do additional screening. We advise: Exercise a high degree of caution in Egypt overall due to the threat of terrorism. Higher levels apply in some areas. Download Egypt Download Africa

themes.heading: Middle East conflict
themes.text: Conflict in the Middle East region has escalated in recent days, with military strikes and reprisal attacks occurring in a number of locations across the region. Prioritise your safety, monitor events and follow local advice - including any warnings to take shelter or avoid locations at high risk of targeting. Conflict in the region may cause flight disruptions and disrupt your travel plans. Airspace closures and flight cancellations can occur with limited notice. The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) has issued advice to air operators to avoid Gulf airspace until at least 29 July 2026. You should check with your travel provider to confirm if your flights are affected. If you travel anywhere with a risk of armed conflict, you may be unable to leave if conflict escalates further. ‘Do not travel’ means ‘do not transit’. ‘Reconsider your need to travel’ also means ‘reconsider your need to transit’. If you need to travel to any level 3 locations, stay as short a time as possible and eliminate unnecessary activities. Advice levels can go up at short notice if conditions deteriorate. For more information, see our page on the Travel and security impacts of conflict in the Middle East and read our travel advice below.

themes.heading: Safety
themes.text: The situation in the Middle East region remains unpredictable. Military conflict in the Middle East has resulted in airspace closures, flight cancellations and other travel disruptions in the region. You should verify the status of any border crossing before you travel by air, land or sea. Terrorism is a threat, including in Egypt. Terrorist attacks could happen at any time or anywhere. Avoid possible targets including venues that draw in foreigners, tourists or crowds where possible Demonstrations and protest activity may also occur, and local security situations could deteriorate with little notice. Avoid all demonstrations and protests. The Rafah crossing between Gaza (Palestine) and Egypt may be closed or have highly restricted access. It can be subject to change at short notice. The Gaza side of the crossing has been subject to air strikes in recent years. For information on the Taba land border crossing between Egypt and Israel, see Travel in 'Full Advice' below. Crime can happen anywhere. Don't leave valuables unsecured in your hotel room or unattended in a public place. Women, particularly foreigners, are frequently subject to unwelcome male attention. Full travel advice: Safety

themes.heading: Health
themes.text: Infectious diseases are common. These include hepatitis, filariasis and rabies. Only drink boiled or bottled water. Avoid contact with dogs and cats. Don't swim in fresh water, including the Nile River, to avoid waterborne diseases such as bilharzia (schistosomiasis). Dengue and malaria are present. Use insect repellent and make sure your accommodation is insect-proof. Cairo can have very high levels of pollution and dust. If you suffer from breathing difficulties or a lung condition, seek medical advice before travel. Full travel advice: Health

themes.heading: Local laws
themes.text: Making public comments that criticise the Egyptian government, security forces, or Islam can be illegal. Police have arrested foreign visitors who posted critical social media. There are severe consequences for carrying illegal drugs, including the death penalty, long prison sentences or deportation. You need approval to bring satellite phones and radio communications equipment into Egypt. Apply to the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology before you leave. The use of drones is illegal. Egyptian family law differs significantly from Australian law. Before you become involved in a local legal matter, get legal advice, including on family and business legal matters. Although same-sex relationships are not explicitly criminalised in Egypt, there is little public acceptance of same-sex relationships. Avoid public displays of affection. Sex outside of marriage is illegal. Dress standards are very conservative, particularly for women. Wear modest clothes that cover your legs and upper arms. Full travel advice: Local laws

themes.heading: Travel
themes.text: Egypt has increased health screening measures at airports and other ports of entry in response to the Ebola outbreak in parts of Africa. If you’re travelling from an Ebola affected country, you may need to do additional screening. For information on Egypt's border measures, see Travel. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Egypt for the latest details. Foreign journalists need accreditation from the Egyptian Press Centre before arrival. There are severe punishments for journalists working without accreditation. There are landmines in some coastal and desert areas. Get advice from local authorities before you travel and do not travel off marked paths or roads. See Landmines in Egypt for further information. Australian-Egyptian dual nationals are treated as Egyptian. This will limit your access to consular assistance. Always travel on your Australian passport if possible. This doesn't mean you will receive special treatment in terms of local law. Full travel advice: Travel

themes.heading: Local contacts
themes.text: The Consular Services Charter tells you what the Australian Government may and can't do to help when you're overseas. For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Cairo. To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy’s social media accounts. Full travel advice: Local contacts

themes.heading: Terrorism
themes.text: Terrorism is a threat worldwide. Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time. Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places. Although the greatest terrorist threat is in North Sinai, terrorist attacks could occur anywhere in Egypt. Targets might include: Egyptian security forces religious sites government buildings large public gatherings tourist locations, including those visited by foreign nationals To stay safe: consider the level of security at places you plan to visit have a clear exit plan in case of a security incident report suspicious activity or items to the police monitor the media for new or emerging threats take official warnings seriously follow the advice of local authorities If there's a terrorist attack, leave the area as soon as it's safe to do so. Continue to avoid the area in case of secondary attacks.

themes.heading: Governorate of North Sinai
themes.text: There's a high threat of terrorist attacks and violent crime in the Governorate of North Sinai, including the Taba-Suez Road. Do not travel to North Sinai. Terrorists or criminals could target you, or you might be included in violence directed at others. There is a significant presence of armed security forces and police in governorates throughout the country, including North Sinai. Curfews may be imposed on very short notice. The Rafah crossing between Gaza (Palestine) and Egypt may be closed or have highly restricted access for long periods and is subject to change at short notice. The Gaza side of the border has been subject to air strikes in recent years. If, despite our advice, you decide to travel to North Sinai: seek professional security advice arrange contingency plans and personal security measures note that our ability to provide consular assistance may be extremely limited More information: Terrorism

themes.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
themes.text: Protests can happen anywhere and at any time, although strict security clampdowns have been preventing protests in recent years. Clashes between rival protesters or security forces have resulted in many deaths and injuries. Foreigners, including journalists, have been among the victims. Security forces have targeted foreign journalists. Egyptian authorities have arrested, detained or questioned journalists.

themes.heading: Protest hotspots
themes.text: The following places and times are common focal points for demonstrations: Tahrir Square and surrounding streets, including the nearby British and US Embassies and Garden City area, in Cairo the Al-Ittihadiya Presidential Palace in Heliopolis, Giza the area of the Raba Al-Adawiya Square in Nasr City Fridays following midday prayers the anniversary of the 2011 revolution on 25 January and the days leading up to this public holiday Under Egyptian law, it's illegal for: more than 10 people to gather without notice foreigners to participate in protests and demonstrations Authorities may arrest foreigners who participate in protests. Egyptian authorities may impose curfews and restrictions on movement at short notice. Public protests and events that draw large groups of people can turn violent. To protect yourself during periods of unrest: avoid demonstrations, rallies and large crowds monitor the media for possible unrest and avoid those areas obey any curfews and respect local laws follow the advice of local authorities be prepared to change your travel plans Protests may disrupt transport. Contact your airline or travel agent to check. More information: Protests and civil unrest

themes.heading: Crime
themes.text: Petty crime is low in Cairo. Violent crime is rare but can occur, including armed robbery, sexual assault, incidents involving minors, carjacking and burglary. Take extra care if you're a woman and alone. Women may be physically and verbally harassed or assaulted, including when using public transport and walking in public areas. Ensure children and young people are always accompanied by known or trusted people and not left alone with hotel or entertainment/excursion staff. If you feel uncomfortable, leave the area and seek help from the hotel or local authorities. The Tourist Police can be contacted on 126 or 122 if you're a resident of Egypt. Taxi and rideshare drivers have assaulted passengers, including foreigners (see Travel). To protect yourself from crime: don't leave valuables outside a safe in your hotel room or unattended in a public place be alert to pickpockets and bag snatchers in tourist areas, particularly after dark read reducing the risk of sexual assault before you go If you're the victim of a crime, report the incident to the tourist police immediately. If you don't report a crime before you leave, you may not be able to seek prosecution later. More information: Advice for women

themes.heading: Kidnapping
themes.text: Kidnapping can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe. Foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped overseas whilst travelling. Kidnapping is a risk in Egypt. There is a threat of terrorist kidnappings in North Sinai. Terrorist attacks have occurred frequently in this area and have targeted foreign nationals. You’re responsible for your own safety. The Australian Government's longstanding policy is that it doesn't make payments or concessions to kidnappers. Ransom payments to kidnappers have funded further terrorist attacks and criminal activity. Ransom payments to terrorist groups are likely to contravene Australian counter-terrorism financing laws. More information: Kidnapping Scams that affect travellers Reducing the risk of piracy

themes.heading: Tours and adventure activities
themes.text: Transport and tour operators don't always follow safety and maintenance standards. This includes for: hot air balloon flights and boat tours water sports, including scuba diving adventure activities, including quad biking and kite surfing If you plan to do an adventure activity: check if your travel insurance policy covers it check tours are well equipped with food, medical supplies and emergency communications ask about and insist on minimum safety requirements always use available safety gear, such as life jackets or seatbelts If proper safety equipment isn't available, use another provider.

themes.heading: Extreme weather and natural disasters
themes.text: Egypt, particularly Cairo, experiences earthquakes. Find out about local safety procedures in case one strikes. Sand and dust storms occur between March and May. If a natural disaster occurs, follow the advice of local authorities. More information: Global Disaster Alert and Coordination System.

themes.heading: Travel insurance
themes.text: Get comprehensive travel insurance before you leave and closely read the Product Disclosure Statement (PDS) for what is and isn’t covered. It must cover all medical costs overseas, including evacuation. The Australian Government will not pay for these costs. If you can’t afford travel insurance, you shouldn’t travel. This is true for everyone, even if you are healthy and fit. If you don’t have insurance, you might have to pay thousands of dollars up front for medical care. Before you travel, check your policy, make sure: you know what activities and medical care your insurance covers, including evacuations your insurance covers you for your whole trip, including any stopovers you know any exclusions – for example, you might need to pay extra for activities like motorcycling, snow sports, scuba diving or bungee jumping. More information: Travel insurance

themes.heading: Physical and mental health
themes.text: Travelling overseas can be stressful and uncertain. You may experience unfamiliar environments, travel disruptions, changes in normal routines and limited access to health care. Not taking your prescribed medications is a common cause of mental ill-health for Australians overseas. If you are concerned about your welfare, or someone else's, contact your nearest Australian embassy, high commission, or consulate. You can also call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305. Mental health resources: Information and advice on mental illness (HealthDirect). Living with mental illness (BeyondBlue) Medicare Mental Health (the Department of Health and Aged Care) More information: General health advice Mental health support overseas

themes.heading: Infectious diseases
themes.text: Waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases are common, including these listed by the World Health Organization: hepatitis filariasis rabies Serious outbreaks sometimes occur. To protect yourself from illness: drink boiled water or bottled water with sealed lids don't swim in fresh water, including the Nile River, to avoid waterborne diseases, such as bilharzia (schistosomiasis) (World Health Organization) avoid contact with dogs and cats Get urgent medical attention if bitten by an animal. Ask your doctor about health risks and vaccines before you go. More information: Infectious diseases

themes.heading: Insect-borne diseases
themes.text: There have been limited dengue outbreaks in recent years. To protect yourself from insect-borne disease: make sure your accommodation is insect-proof use insect repellent wear long, loose, light-coloured clothing

themes.heading: Medical care
themes.text: The standard of medical facilities in Cairo is enough for routine illnesses. Elsewhere, facilities can be very basic. Treatment can also be costly. Many require up-front payment. If you become seriously ill or injured, you'll need to be evacuated to a place with better facilities. Medical evacuation can be very expensive and hard to organise. Ensure you have sufficient travel insurance to cover the costs. There’s no agreement between Australia and Egypt for free healthcare. Tourists must pay for all medical care, even if they need to stay in hospital or be evacuated. This can be very expensive. Make sure your travel insurance covers you. The Australian Government won't pay for these costs.

themes.heading: Drugs
themes.text: Possessing illegal drugs can lead to the death penalty, long prison sentences or deportation. More information: Carrying or using drugs

themes.heading: LGBTQIA+ laws
themes.text: LGBTQIA+ individuals face significant social stigma and discrimination in Egypt. Egyptian law does not explicitly criminalise same-sex relationships, however, people have been charged with 'committing an indecent act in public and breaching public morality' and penalised with imprisonment. LGBTQIA+ people and advocacy groups have reported harassment, intimidation, arrests, and other forms of abuse, including by police. There are also reports that authorities have used social media, dating websites, and mobile phone apps to entrap people suspected of being homosexual or transgender in the act of 'debauchery,' which is a criminal offence that carries severe sentences. There is little public acceptance of homosexuality in Egypt. More information: Advice for LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Dual nationality
themes.text: If you're an Australian-Egyptian dual national, local authorities will treat you as an Egyptian, even if you travel on an Australian passport. This limits our consular services if you're arrested or detained. If possible, always travel on your Australian passport. Dual nationals living in Egypt for long periods need proof of Egyptian citizenship, such as a national identification card. Male dual nationals who haven't completed military service usually don't need to enlist. However, they must get an exemption certificate before they can leave Egypt. Get one from the nearest Egyptian embassy, consulate, or Ministry of Defence Draft Office. If you're arrested, request local authorities inform the Australian Embassy. More information: Advice for dual nationals

themes.heading: Ramadan
themes.text: The Islamic holy month of Ramadan is observed in Egypt. Respect religious and cultural customs and laws during this time. Avoid eating, drinking or smoking in public or in front of people who are fasting. Orthodox Easter is observed by the Christian community, as is Christmas Day, which is celebrated on 7 January in Egypt. The work week is Sunday to Thursday. Egypt's customs, laws, and regulations follow Islamic practices and beliefs. Exercise common sense and discretion in dress and behaviour. Dress conservatively. Knee-length or longer dresses and long sleeves are preferable for women, and men should not wear shorts outside tourist areas. Respect religious and social traditions to avoid offending local sensitivities. Overt public displays of intimate affection are frowned upon in Egyptian culture. If in doubt, seek local advice.

themes.heading: Egypt border
themes.text: Borders can open or close, and entry and exit requirements may change without notice, especially during a conflict. Confirm requirements with local authorities. Check the latest information from local authorities on entry requirements and border arrangements. Plan for possible delays at border crossings. Make sure you have all documentation, and enough food, water, cash and medication.

themes.heading: Border with Israel
themes.text: The Taba border crossing (Menachem Begin Crossing) between Israel and Egypt is open. The crossing may close or have restricted hours at short notice. If you're departing Israel to Egypt, check the latest public information from local authorities in both Israel and Egypt to determine if border crossings are open before travel. To avoid long wait times at the border, pay the Israeli crossing fee online official website (accessible in Israel only) before you arrive. Your purchase is only valid for the date you paid for and can't be changed or refunded. Plan for possible delays and make sure you have a supply of food, water and medication. You must pay for a tourist visa and a land border entry fee to enter Egypt. If you're planning to travel onward from Taba or Sharm El-Sheikh to Cairo, you'll need to pay an additional fee. Payments must be made in US dollars cash, card payments are not accepted. Travellers should obtain USD before arriving at the crossing. There are ATMs at the border, but they are not always operating.

themes.heading: Visas and border measures
themes.text: Each country or territory decides its own entry and exit rules, including health border screening measures. To find out what you need to enter a destination, ask their embassy, consulate or immigration office.

themes.heading: Visas
themes.text: Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. Contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Egypt for the latest details. To pay for a tourist visa on entry, you must have a non-Egyptian credit card or US dollars in cash. Bring the required amount, as ATMs may not disperse USD or other foreign currency. More information: Egypt Electronic Visa Portal

themes.heading: Yellow fever vaccination
themes.text: You need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Egypt if you arrive from a country where yellow fever is widespread. Some airlines may want to see one when you leave. Find out about returning to Australia after exposure to yellow fever. More information: Countries where yellow fever is a risk

themes.heading: Lost or stolen passport
themes.text: Your passport is valuable and can be used by others to commit crimes. Don’t let anyone trick you into giving it to them. Always keep it in a safe place. If your passport is lost or stolen, tell the Australian Government: In Australia: contact the Australian Passport Information Service. Overseas: contact the nearest Australian embassy or consulate.

themes.heading: Passport with ‘X’ gender identifier
themes.text: Australian passports comply with international standards for sex and gender. If your passport has an 'X' in the sex field, we can’t guarantee your entry or transit to other countries. Ask the embassy or consulate of your destinations if they’ll accept your passport with an 'X' gender marker. Check all transit destinations too. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Money
themes.text: The local currency is the Egyptian Pound (EGP). You can take up to EGP 5000 in cash when travelling to or from Egypt. You must declare all foreign currency amounts over $US 10,000 or equivalent. This covers all forms of currency, not only cash. If you're visiting as a tourist, you may need to pay $US or euros for your accommodation. Most well-established hotels and resorts accept card payments. ATMs are available in most established areas. Consult your financial institution prior to your travel. More information: Embassy or Consulate of Egypt

themes.heading: Landmines
themes.text: There are landmines in some areas, notably: the desert areas around El Alamein stretches of coastline near Marsa Matruh the western shore of the Gulf of Suez the Sinai Peninsula Before you go, tell local authorities of your planned travel. Ask them about current risks and precautions for your route and destination. Do not travel off marked paths or roads.

themes.heading: Travel restrictions and disruptions
themes.text: If you travel around Egypt, you may be stopped at military and civilian checkpoints. Officials at checkpoints have detained and harassed foreigners.

themes.heading: Land borders
themes.text: Egypt's borders are under military control. The military restricts and, in some cases, bans the movement of civilians and vehicles. You need permission to cross borders on the main sealed roads, including at the borders with Libya, Sudan, Israel and parts of the Sinai. Get permission from the Travel Permits Department of the Egyptian Ministry of the Interior.

themes.heading: Other borders
themes.text: Road travel to Abu Simbel, 40km north of the Sudanese border, can be dangerous. If you do go, only travel with an organised tour guarded by police escorts. There's a high threat of terrorist attacks within 40km of Egypt's border with Libya. Deadly attacks have occurred in the area.

themes.heading: Driving permit
themes.text: You can't drive in Egypt on your Australian driver's licence. Before arriving in Egypt, get an international driver's permit and get an embassy or consulate of Egypt to certify it.

themes.heading: Road travel
themes.text: Road travel can be dangerous. Road conditions are very poor. Cars, buses and trucks frequently drive at high speed and without headlights at night. Road accidents occur often. Where possible, avoid travelling by road. Visit regional places, including Luxor, by other means. More information: Driving or riding

themes.heading: Taxis and rideshare
themes.text: Cairo and Alexandria have a lot of taxis. Internationally recognised rideshare apps are available in Cairo and Alexandria. In Cairo, taxis are white. In Alexandria, taxis are black and yellow. All taxis should have a meter. The law requires drivers to use their meters. However, many taxi drivers will claim that the meter is broken and try to negotiate a fare. Taxis rarely have seatbelts, especially in the back seats. Sexual harassment of women by taxi drivers is common. Avoid taxis, especially if you're a woman and on your own. Rideshare apps may be safer as you can track your driver's details and share the trip details with others. If you use a taxi, travel with people you know or advise others of your planned travel and destination.

themes.heading: Public transport
themes.text: The Cairo Metro subway system is generally reliable. Maintenance and safety standards of other public road and rail transport are very poor. More information: Transport and getting around safely

themes.heading: Sea travel
themes.text: Piracy and armed robbery are risks in the southern Red Sea and Gulf of Aden. All forms of shipping are attractive targets for pirates. This includes commercial vessels, pleasure craft and luxury cruise liners. The International Maritime Bureau issues piracy reports. If you plan to travel by boat, be highly alert and cautious in the southern Red Sea and Gulf of Aden.

themes.heading: Air travel
themes.text: DFAT doesn’t give advice about how safe different airlines or flight paths are. Safety risks and weather events may cause flight delays and cancellations. Contact your travel agent or airline to confirm your plans. Airlines and airports can restrict what you can take in your luggage. Regulations may be different to those in Australia. Check with your airline before you travel. Check Egypt's air safety profile with the Aviation Safety Network. More information: Air travel

themes.heading: Consular contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. For consular help, contact:

themes.heading: Australian Embassy, Cairo
themes.text: 11th floor, World Trade Centre 1191 Corniche el Nil Boulaq, Cairo, Egypt Phone: +20 2 2770 6600 Email: cairo.austremb@dfat.gov.au Website: egypt.embassy.gov.au Facebook: Australia in Egypt X: @AusAmbEGY The Australian Embassy in Cairo operates on Sunday – Thursday. The security situation may affect Embassy opening hours.

themes.heading: 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre
themes.text: In a consular emergency, if you can't contact an embassy, call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on: +61 2 6261 3305 from overseas 1300 555 135 in Australia
